ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for low-carb gyudon or gyudon with tofu substitution. FOODON_03311737 (beef dish) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef-based composite dish — mixed preparation with vegetables and tofu; gyudon variant without rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for gyudon or low-carb gyudon. A00LC (composite beef dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Tofu substitution for rice is a non-standard preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for gyudon or low-carb beef bowl dish. SNOMED CT does not index prepared composite dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 420 kcal, 35 g protein, 22 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); Tofu, raw, firm (FDC ID 172475); Soy sauce (FDC ID 172238); Mirin (FDC ID 169820); Salad greens composite (FDC ID 169247)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for low-carb gyudon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-weighted averages: approximately 45% thinly sliced simmered beef (USDA FDC 174033), 35% firm tofu (USDA FDC 172475), 10% simmering sauce (soy sauce, mirin, dashi — USDA FDC 172238, 169820), and 10% fresh salad with sesame dressing (USDA FDC 169247). Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water set to -1 as detailed composite breakdown is not available from a single approved source entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: tofu GI ~15, simmered beef GI ~0, mixed dish weighted estimate ~35.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-carb gyudon. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on weighted component analysis using University of Sydney GI Database values: tofu (GI ~15), simmered beef (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate), simmering sauce with mirin/sugar (GI ~55 for mirin, small proportion), and fresh salad greens (GI ~15). Weighted average across 400 g serving with 20 g total carbohydrate yields GL = (35 × 20) / 100 = 7.0 per serving. Analogous estimate per Atkinson et al. 2008 methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce preparation and limit sesame dressing to reduce sodium load; the tofu and salad greens provide meaningful potassium offset but do not fully compensate for the sauce-derived sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g; 20 g per 400 g serving) with tofu replacing rice, yielding an estimated glycemic load of 7 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: This low-carb gyudon is a suitable option for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low glycemic load of approximately 7 per serving; patients should request reduced-sugar simmering sauce and plain sesame oil dressing to minimise added sugar from mirin and commercial dressings.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.25 g/100 g and trans fat 0.13 g/100 g from beef; sodium 300 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving provides 9 g saturated fat and 0.52 g trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to limit saturated fat and trans fat intake from the beef component, and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium; the tofu and salad greens contribute favourably to the lipid profile and do not raise cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef contains arachidonic acid which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6); tomato as a nightshade vegetable may trigger solanine-related inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with tofu or salmon to eliminate arachidonic acid and potentially add omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit; nightshade-sensitive individuals should request omission of tomato.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Carb Gyudon (低糖質牛丼, Teitōshitsu Gyūdon) is a Japanese-inspired beef bowl dish adapted for low-carbohydrate dietary needs, commonly served in Singapore's Japanese food delivery and restaurant market. Unlike traditional gyudon which is served over steamed white rice, this variant replaces the rice base with silken or firm tofu and is accompanied by a fresh salad with sesame dressing. The beef component consists of thinly sliced beef simmered in a savoury Japanese sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, dashi stock, and onion, producing a sweet-savoury umami flavour profile characteristic of gyudon. The tofu base absorbs the simmering sauce and provides a soft, silky texture contrast to the tender beef slices. The fresh salad component adds crunch and freshness, with sesame dressing contributing nuttiness. Per 100 g, this dish provides approximately 105 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 5.5 g fat (2.25 g saturated), 5.0 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. A standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 420 kcal and 20 g carbohydrate, yielding an estimated glycemic load of 7 — classified as low. This dish is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low glycemic load and high protein content from both beef and tofu. Sodium content is moderate and warrants attention for hypertension patients. The saturated fat and trans fat from beef are relevant for cardiovascular disease risk management. The dish is dairy-free and contains soy and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from component-weighted USDA FoodData Central data for beef, tofu, soy sauce, mirin, and salad greens. No single approved source entry exists for low-carb gyudon as a composite dish. Input values from the original record (confidence 0.7) are consistent with the component-weighted estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database component values. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this composite low-carb gyudon dish (beef + tofu + salad + sesame dressing) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Component-level data exists but composite dish-level values for these fields cannot be reliably derived without a full recipe analysis. Two portions in the input both labelled is_default=true; the canonical default serving is 400 g (1 bowl). The dish is a Singapore-market adaptation of Japanese gyudon and is not a traditional Japanese preparation; country_of_origin is Singapore. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. This is a Japanese cuisine dish; mandatory multilingual requirement is Japanese only (G6). Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not mandatory for Japanese cuisine and are correctly left null. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Nutrient source updated to reference only USDA FoodData Central approved entries; forbidden delivery-platform source removed.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Machine key type2_diabetes used.
